content.text: Streaming audio is the continuous, low-latency transmission of audio data between a browser and a server — or between microservices — as a sequence of small, timed chunks rather than a single large file. In voice agent systems, two concurrent streams run simultaneously: an
carries the user's microphone audio to the server for speech recognition, and an
carries synthesised speech from the server back to the browser for playback.
The fundamental unit of work is the

: a fixed-size slice of raw PCM samples that gets processed and transmitted repeatedly at a constant rate. WebRTC systems standardise on 20 ms frames (320 samples at 16 kHz). The Web Audio API schedules rendering in 128-sample blocks. Bridging those two granularities — and doing so without glitches or perceptible delay — is the central engineering problem of real-time audio.
Three layers stack on top of each other: the
compresses raw PCM (PCM16, G.711, Opus); the
moves frames across the network (WebSocket binary messages or WebRTC data channels); and the

absorbs jitter and schedules playback (jitter buffers, AudioBufferSourceNode chains). Understanding each layer independently — and how they couple — is the prerequisite for building a stable voice agent.

Voice is the highest-bandwidth, most latency-sensitive modality in human communication. A delay of 150 ms is barely perceptible in conversation; 300 ms begins to feel awkward; anything above 500 ms breaks turn-taking entirely. For a voice agent to feel natural rather than laggy, every component of the pipeline — capture, encoding, transmission, recognition, generation, decoding, and playback — must complete end-to-end within that 300–500 ms budget.

Getting streaming audio wrong produces failures that are immediately and viscerally obvious to users: choppy or robotic speech, words getting cut off at the end of utterances, echo or reverb artifacts, silence gaps between TTS sentences, or the agent speaking over the user because barge-in detection failed. These failures erode user trust faster than almost any other failure mode in an AI product.
Streaming audio is also where many of the interesting technical constraints in real-time AI systems become concrete. The pipeline forces you to understand
(what happens when the server generates TTS faster than the network can carry it),
(capture and playback clocks running at slightly different rates),
(how to conceal a missing 20 ms frame), and
(deciding when the user has finished speaking). All of these problems must be solved before the STT and LLM components even come into play.

Because TTS output is streamed chunk by chunk, the browser can begin playing the first sentence while the LLM is still generating the second. This can reduce perceived response latency by 500–1000 ms compared to waiting for the full TTS response before playback begins.

Streaming keeps only a small rolling buffer in memory at any moment. A 30-minute voice call streamed in 20 ms chunks never allocates more than a few kilobytes for the audio data itself, regardless of total call duration.
Streaming STT services like Deepgram and AssemblyAI return partial transcripts as they receive audio, enabling the LLM to begin planning a response before the user has finished speaking.

Because the capture stream runs continuously, the server can detect that the user has started speaking mid-response and abort TTS playback. This requires the audio pipeline to be always-on, which streaming architecture enables.

The same streaming abstraction works over WebSockets (most voice agent deployments), WebRTC (when peer-to-peer or NAT traversal matters), and even HTTP/2 server-sent events for playback-only use cases.
The primary cost of streaming is

. A non-streaming approach — record a full utterance, send it, receive a full response, play it — can be implemented with a few dozen lines of JavaScript. A production-grade streaming pipeline requires careful orchestration of audio contexts, worklets, WebSocket framing, server-side asyncio queues, and a playback scheduler. Each component has its own failure modes, and they interact in non-obvious ways.

WebSocket operates over TCP, which reorders and retransmits dropped segments. A single retransmit can stall the receive buffer by 50–200 ms, causing a gap in playback even though no data was actually lost. Jitter buffers compensate by introducing a small playback delay (30–80 ms) to absorb bursts.

The browser's AudioContext clock and the server's system clock are independent. Over a long call they drift apart by tens of milliseconds per minute. The playback scheduler must correct for this by measuring the actual AudioContext currentTime against expected timestamps.

Browsers suspend the AudioContext when the tab is backgrounded or when the user has not yet interacted with the page. Audio playback silently stops. The agent must detect suspension (via the statechange event) and resume it on the next user interaction.

If the TTS engine generates audio faster than the WebSocket can drain, the ws.bufferedAmount grows unboundedly. Without explicit backpressure gating, the server queues megabytes of audio, all of which plays out after the user has already moved on.

On a device without hardware echo cancellation, the microphone captures the speaker output, feeding it back into the STT stream. getUserMedia with echoCancellation: true mitigates this in most browsers but not on all hardware.
, which returns a MediaStream attached to the microphone. That stream is connected to an
running inside a dedicated audio thread. The worklet's

method fires every 128 samples (about 2.9 ms at 44.1 kHz or 8 ms at 16 kHz), converting Float32 samples to Int16 PCM and posting them back to the main thread. The main thread accumulates 320 samples (one 20 ms frame at 16 kHz) before forwarding the chunk over a WebSocket as a binary message.
On the server, an asyncio WebSocket handler receives binary messages and places them on an

. A consumer coroutine drains that queue, feeding chunks to the STT engine's streaming API. As partial transcripts arrive, a second coroutine feeds them to the LLM. As the LLM streams tokens, a third coroutine pipes text to the TTS engine and receives back PCM chunks, which are immediately sent back over the WebSocket.
On the playback side, the browser's WebSocket
handler decodes the incoming binary data (Int16 → Float32), creates an
, and schedules it on the AudioContext using
. Each chunk's start time is set to the exact AudioContext time at which the previous chunk ends, producing seamless, gap-free playback regardless of when the WebSocket message actually arrived.

attrs.alt: Streaming audio pipeline: Microphone feeds AudioWorklet which produces PCM16 chunks that are sent over WebSocket to a server Jitter Buffer, then through STT to LLM Agent to TTS Engine, whose PCM output is queued and sent back over WebSocket to a browser Playback Queue and AudioBufferSourceNode, finally reaching the Speaker.

content.text: The diagram shows the full round-trip pipeline. The
(top row, left to right) runs from the microphone through an AudioWorkletProcessor that emits 128-sample frames, accumulated into 20 ms PCM16 chunks and sent as binary WebSocket messages. The

(middle band, right to left) reorders incoming chunks in a jitter buffer, streams them to STT, pipes the transcript to the LLM, and streams LLM tokens to TTS, queueing resulting PCM chunks for retransmission. The
(bottom row, left to right) receives binary frames, decodes them to Float32, and schedules AudioBufferSourceNodes so each chunk begins exactly where the previous one ended.
Use streaming audio for any voice interface where end-to-end latency must stay under 500 ms — call centre bots, voice assistants, real-time translation, interactive tutoring.
Use streaming audio when barge-in is required: the always-on capture stream is necessary to detect that the user has started speaking while the agent is still playing a response.
Use streaming TTS output (even without streaming capture) whenever the TTS response will exceed 3–5 seconds — the first-word latency improvement alone justifies the complexity.

Prefer non-streaming (push-to-talk, file upload) when latency is not a concern, the deployment environment lacks reliable WebSocket support, or the conversation model is turn-based enough that full-utterance processing is acceptable.

Use WebRTC instead of WebSocket when the deployment involves peer-to-peer audio (device to device without a server relay), when you need built-in DTLS/SRTP encryption, or when the network path involves symmetric NAT where TCP connections are unreliable.
Request microphone access and set up the AudioContext with the target sample rate (16 kHz for STT-optimised pipelines):
Register an AudioWorklet module (a separate JS file) that implements
method, convert Float32 input samples to Int16 and post them to the main thread via
In the main thread, accumulate posted Int16 arrays until you have enough samples for one 20 ms frame (320 samples at 16 kHz). Send the accumulated buffer over a WebSocket as a binary message.
On the server (Python asyncio), create a WebSocket endpoint. Consume incoming binary messages, push them onto an
, and run a producer coroutine that drains the queue and feeds chunks to the STT streaming API.

Pipe STT transcript events to the LLM with streaming enabled. As token chunks arrive, forward them to the TTS API's streaming endpoint. Send the resulting PCM chunks back over the WebSocket as binary messages immediately upon receipt, rather than buffering the full TTS response.
In the browser, gate WebSocket sends on
: if it exceeds a threshold (e.g. 8192 bytes), drop or defer the current chunk to prevent queue buildup.
. For each incoming PCM chunk, create an AudioBuffer, fill it with decoded Float32 samples, create an AudioBufferSourceNode, call
by the buffer's duration. This chain produces seamless playback.
Server: Python asyncio WebSocket Audio Streaming

# server.py — asyncio WebSocket server for a voice agent pipeline
# pip install websockets deepgram-sdk openai

import asyncio
import websockets
from deepgram import DeepgramClient, LiveTranscriptionEvents, LiveOptions
import openai

DEEPGRAM_API_KEY = "your-deepgram-key"
OPENAI_API_KEY = "your-openai-key"

openai_client = openai.AsyncOpenAI(api_key=OPENAI_API_KEY)


async def stream_tts_to_websocket(
    text: str,
    websocket: websockets.WebSocketServerProtocol
) -> None:
    """Stream TTS PCM16 audio chunks back to the browser."""
    async with openai_client.audio.speech.with_streaming_response.create(
        model="tts-1",
        voice="alloy",
        input=text,
        response_format="pcm",  # raw 24 kHz PCM16
    ) as response:
        async for chunk in response.iter_bytes(chunk_size=4096):
            if chunk:
                # Backpressure: yield to event loop between sends
                await websocket.send(chunk)
                await asyncio.sleep(0)  # cooperative yield


async def handle_voice_session(
    websocket: websockets.WebSocketServerProtocol
) -> None:
    """Manage a single voice agent session."""
    audio_queue: asyncio.Queue[bytes] = asyncio.Queue(maxsize=200)
    transcript_parts: list[str] = []

    # --- Deepgram live transcription setup ---
    dg_client = DeepgramClient(api_key=DEEPGRAM_API_KEY)
    dg_connection = dg_client.listen.asyncwebsocket.v("1")

    final_transcript: asyncio.Future[str] = asyncio.get_event_loop().create_future()

    async def on_transcript(self, result, **kwargs):
        sentence = result.channel.alternatives[0].transcript
        if result.is_final and sentence:
            transcript_parts.append(sentence)
        if result.speech_final:
            # User stopped speaking — resolve the future
            if not final_transcript.done():
                final_transcript.set_result(" ".join(transcript_parts))

    dg_connection.on(LiveTranscriptionEvents.Transcript, on_transcript)

    options = LiveOptions(
        model="nova-2",
        language="en-US",
        smart_format=True,
        encoding="linear16",
        channels=1,
        sample_rate=16000,
        interim_results=True,
        utterance_end_ms="1000",
        vad_events=True,
    )
    await dg_connection.start(options)

    # --- Feed audio queue to Deepgram ---
    async def send_audio_to_deepgram():
        while True:
            chunk = await audio_queue.get()
            if chunk is None:  # sentinel — session ending
                break
            await dg_connection.send(chunk)

    feeder_task = asyncio.create_task(send_audio_to_deepgram())

    try:
        # --- Main session loop ---
        async for message in websocket:
            if isinstance(message, bytes):
                # PCM16 audio from the browser — queue it for STT
                try:
                    audio_queue.put_nowait(message)
                except asyncio.QueueFull:
                    pass  # drop chunk rather than block

            elif isinstance(message, str) and message == "__end__":
                # User indicated end of turn
                transcript_parts.clear()
                if not final_transcript.done():
                    final_transcript.cancel()
                final_transcript = asyncio.get_event_loop().create_future()

            # When Deepgram signals speech_final, respond
            if final_transcript.done() and not final_transcript.cancelled():
                user_text = final_transcript.result()
                final_transcript = asyncio.get_event_loop().create_future()
                transcript_parts.clear()

                # LLM — streaming, but we collect full response for TTS
                # (or pipe token-by-token to a streaming TTS that supports it)
                llm_response = await openai_client.chat.completions.create(
                    model="gpt-4o-mini",
                    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_text}],
                    stream=False,
                )
                reply_text = llm_response.choices[0].message.content
                await stream_tts_to_websocket(reply_text, websocket)

    finally:
        audio_queue.put_nowait(None)
        await feeder_task
        await dg_connection.finish()


async def main():
    async with websockets.serve(handle_voice_session, "0.0.0.0", 8765):
        await asyncio.Future()  # run forever


if __name__ == "__main__":
    asyncio.run(main())

Consider a healthcare triage voice agent that handles after-hours patient calls. Patients call on a standard phone (PSTN), which is bridged to a WebRTC gateway running Twilio or Vonage. The gateway converts G.711 μ-law audio (8 kHz, 8-bit) to PCM16 at 16 kHz and streams it over a WebSocket to the agent server.

The server pipeline is implemented with Python asyncio and Deepgram Nova-2 for streaming STT. Because patient voices on phone lines are often noisy and accented, Deepgram's real-time VAD (voice activity detection) model is used to detect utterance boundaries instead of relying on fixed silence thresholds. When Deepgram emits a

event, the transcript is sent to a Claude-3-Haiku model with a triage-specific system prompt. The LLM response is piped to OpenAI TTS-1 in streaming mode, and each 4096-byte PCM chunk is sent back over the WebSocket as soon as it arrives.
On the outbound (playback) path, the WebRTC gateway receives PCM16 chunks from the WebSocket and converts them to G.711 for the phone network. The key engineering decision here is

: the gateway maintains a 120 ms adaptive jitter buffer to absorb server-side processing variability, while keeping the target end-to-end latency below 350 ms. During a 60-day production pilot across 12,000 calls, the primary reliability issue observed was the Deepgram WebSocket disconnecting after 90 minutes without a keepalive ping — fixed by sending a binary
The MediaRecorder API is convenient and widely documented, but it was designed for recording to file, not real-time streaming. Its minimum

argument is nominally 1 ms but in practice rarely delivers chunks below 100–200 ms, and it encodes to Opus or WebM by default rather than raw PCM, which most STT APIs do not accept without transcoding. Use AudioWorkletProcessor for any pipeline requiring sub-50 ms capture latency.
Browsers require a user gesture before they will start an AudioContext. A context created before any click or keypress enters the suspended state and silently produces no output. Always call
inside a user event handler, and listen for
to detect if the context is later suspended by the browser (e.g. due to inactivity in a background tab).
On the capture path, the AudioWorklet fires 125 times per second. If the WebSocket is slow to drain — due to a loaded server or a congested network — the

grows silently. After 5–10 seconds, the browser may be holding 50–100 KB of queued audio that the server will eventually process, but by then the user has moved on and the STT output will arrive far out of sync with the conversation. Gate every send on
; drop or pause capture if the threshold is exceeded.

Waiting for the entire TTS response before sending the first byte to the browser adds the TTS generation latency (often 500–2000 ms for a typical sentence) to the user-perceived response time. Stream TTS output chunk-by-chunk from the server to the browser. Even a 3-second TTS response feels fast if the first 200 ms of audio arrives 100 ms after the LLM starts generating.
Web Audio uses Float32 normalised to
. STT and TTS APIs expect Int16 PCM (range
). The conversion factor is 32767 (not 32768) when encoding, and
when decoding. Getting this slightly wrong produces audio at the correct pitch but with clipping or attenuation artifacts.

Set AudioContext to 16 kHz if your STT API prefers 16 kHz. Letting the browser resample silently from 48 kHz to 16 kHz inside AudioContext produces better quality than resampling in JavaScript. Never mix sample rates across the pipeline without explicit resampling code.
Size chunks to match STT frame expectations.

Most streaming STT APIs process audio in 20 ms or 30 ms frames internally. Sending 20 ms chunks (320 samples at 16 kHz) aligns with this boundary and minimises internal buffering latency at the server.
Use transferable objects for AudioWorklet messages.
transfers ownership of the ArrayBuffer rather than copying it, cutting the memory allocation to zero and keeping the audio thread's GC pressure minimal.
Flush the playback queue on barge-in.
When the user starts speaking mid-response, call
to stop all scheduled AudioBufferSourceNodes and reset
. Also send an abort signal to the server so it stops TTS generation.
Keep WebSocket connections alive with pings.
Long-lived WebSocket connections (>60 s) are silently dropped by load balancers and proxies. Send a periodic ping from both client and server. On the server side:

Instrument four numbers per session: capture chunk rate (should be 50 chunks/s), server queue depth (should stay near 0), TTS-first-byte latency, and playback buffer depth. Alerting on queue depth spikes catches backpressure problems before users notice audio degradation.

Mobile networks drop WebSocket connections frequently. On disconnect, the client should pause capture, buffer new audio locally for up to 2 s, re-establish the WebSocket, and resume streaming. The server session state (conversation history) should be persisted separately so the reconnect does not lose context.

A single voice session consumes approximately 256 kbps of inbound bandwidth (PCM16, 16 kHz, mono, 20 ms frames), and 384–512 kbps outbound (24 kHz TTS PCM). With Opus compression — available on WebRTC or if the STT API accepts Opus — inbound bandwidth drops to 24–32 kbps. For a server handling 1,000 concurrent sessions, the bandwidth difference between PCM16 and Opus is roughly 220 Mbps vs 32 Mbps.

On the server side, the asyncio event loop is well-suited for high-concurrency WebSocket sessions because each session spends most of its time waiting on I/O (STT API, LLM API, TTS API). A single Python process can typically handle 50–200 concurrent sessions before the CPU-bound STT decoding saturates a core. For higher scale, run multiple Python workers behind a WebSocket-aware load balancer (HAProxy or NGINX with
), and pin each session to a single worker using consistent hashing on a session ID cookie.

If the server audio queue depth exceeds 30 chunks (600 ms), the session has fallen behind real time. Shed load by temporarily dropping every other chunk (halving the STT quality is usually preferable to total failure).

Always use async HTTP clients (httpx with asyncio) for TTS API calls. A blocking HTTP request inside an asyncio coroutine stalls the entire event loop thread, introducing 100–500 ms latency spikes across all sessions.

Creating a new AudioContext per TTS response (a common mistake) creates and destroys audio threads on every response, causing 50–100 ms gaps. Create one AudioContext for the session lifetime and reuse the playback scheduler.
Low-latency audio I/O in a dedicated audio rendering thread. Preferred over ScriptProcessorNode (deprecated).
AudioBufferSourceNode chain for gapless playback; AudioContext.currentTime for scheduling.
Pure-asyncio WebSocket server. Binary message support. Handles ping/pong keepalive automatically.
Streaming ASR with speech_final events, built-in VAD, word-level timestamps. Supports PCM16 + Opus.
WebSocket-based streaming STT. Alternative to Deepgram; comparable latency.
Streaming PCM or MP3 chunks via response.iter_bytes(). Low-latency option for voice agents.
Higher quality voices; WebSocket streaming API for lowest latency. PCM or MP3 output.
Peer-to-peer or server-relayed; includes AEC, built-in Opus, DTLS/SRTP. Higher complexity than WebSocket.
Voice streams must be encrypted in transit. Use
(WebSocket over TLS) for all production deployments — never

. Terminate TLS at a load balancer or reverse proxy (NGINX, Caddy) that holds the certificate, and allow plaintext WebSocket only on the loopback interface between the proxy and the application server.

Validate a session token (JWT or signed cookie) in the WebSocket HTTP handshake, before any audio data is accepted. An unauthenticated WebSocket endpoint that accepts binary data is trivially exploitable for resource exhaustion — a single client can send audio at full rate indefinitely.

Reject binary frames larger than the expected chunk size (e.g. 640 bytes for a 20 ms PCM16 mono frame). Drop sessions that exceed a maximum ingestion rate (e.g. > 60 frames/s) to prevent amplification attacks where a client sends audio faster than real-time to flood the STT API.

Each session's asyncio.Queue must be private to that session. A shared global queue would leak audio between users and create a denial-of-service vector where one fast sender starves all other sessions.
Handle GDPR and data retention for audio.

Audio data is personal data under GDPR. Do not log raw audio payloads by default. If call recording is required for quality assurance, store it in an encrypted object store with per-user access controls and a defined retention policy. Transcript text (which does not contain biometric voice characteristics) is usually lower-risk to log.

Streaming APIs are stateful and can disconnect mid-session. The server pipeline must catch WebSocket close events from the STT and TTS providers, send a user-facing error message (or a pre-recorded fallback TTS clip), and offer the option to retry rather than silently hanging the session.

Use AudioWorkletProcessor — not MediaRecorder — for real-time capture; it delivers sub-10 ms frame latency and raw PCM output, and operates on a dedicated audio rendering thread that cannot be blocked by main-thread JavaScript.

Stream TTS output chunk-by-chunk and schedule each chunk with AudioBufferSourceNode.start(nextPlayTime); this produces seamless, gap-free audio and reduces user-perceived latency by 500–1000 ms compared to waiting for a full response before playback.

Gate WebSocket sends on ws.bufferedAmount on the capture path and use asyncio.Queue with a maxsize on the server side; without explicit backpressure management, slow networks silently accumulate hundreds of milliseconds of queued audio that arrives far out of sync.
Standardise on 20 ms / 320-sample PCM16 frames at 16 kHz for the capture path; this matches the internal frame size of most streaming STT APIs and keeps the pipeline clock-aligned end-to-end.

Handle AudioContext suspension explicitly — browsers suspend the context on user inactivity and tab backgrounding; listen for onstatechange and call audioCtx.resume() inside any user gesture handler to avoid silent audio stalls.

Encrypt all voice traffic with wss:// (TLS) and authenticate the WebSocket handshake with a session token before accepting audio; an open unauthenticated audio endpoint is a resource exhaustion vulnerability.

Monitor queue depth, chunk arrival rate, and TTS first-byte latency in real time; queue depth spikes are the earliest observable signal of backpressure problems, and catching them before the buffer overflows is the difference between degraded quality and a completely broken session.
